free_text: Source
of ASSUMED value, see EXFOR 21981003.
Pulse height defect in an ionization chamber invest-
igated by cold fission measurements.
G.Simon, J.Trochon, F.Brisard
C.Signarbieux and experimental site
Saclay reactor ORPHEE.
.Thermal reactor spectrum.
For each analyzed fission event four quantities
are recorded, the anode pulse heights and the electron
drift times from each side of the chamber, from which
the fragment mass and energy can be determined
providing that the non-ionizing part of the energy
lost by the fragment (the so-called pulse height
defect) is properly estimated. This was investigated
by considering those fission events for which there
is perfect fragment identification (so-called 'cold'
fission, characterized by high kinetic energy
fragments and no neutron emission). The resulting
pulse height defect was shown to be negligible for
the present configuration, thus the fragment kinetic
energy can be well represented by a linear function
of anode pulse height response.
gas at 450 +-1 mbar pressure, consisting of two back-
to-back gridded chambers separated by a central
cathode at the center of which is located the fissile
sample. Two Frisch grids of 30 micron tungsten wire
screen the anodes from the positive ion effect. The
chamber is operated at constant room temperature, and
the gas filling replenished at a flow rate of 5 l/hr.
The energy resolution was approximately 200 keV for a
100 MeV fragment kinetic energy.
.A very thin fissile sample is required. The experiment
used a sample consisting of UF4 in a layer of
5.3 micro-g/cm**2 evaporated onto a 5 micro-g/cm**2
thick carbon foil backing.
.The fragment kinetic energy and mass before and after
neutron emission are calculated for each fission
event by an iterative procedure, the mean values and
standard deviations are derived from the resulting
distributions. In these calculations the coefficients
in the linear function relating kinetic energy to
pulse height have been adjusted to reproduce the
ASSUMED value.
Total kin.energy before neutron emission.
-The analysis includes neutron emission and backing
effect corrections, the latter to correct for the
energy lost by the fragment that crosses the carbon
foil backing (at zero entrance angle this is about
300 keV compared with the average energy lost in the
fissile deposit of 60 keV, which is negligible).
The accuracy in the assumed value was used to deter-
mine the relative uncertainty in a single absolute
kinetic energy measurement; The uncertainties varied
from DE/E = 3.0E-3 to 4.1E-3 for kinetic energies
ranging from 100 down to 60 MeV.
S.W.

Misprints in ASSUNED were corrected (two brackets were
deleted). Second reference C,85SANTA,1,393,1985 was
move in REL-REF. Subents 009-010 were added.
DATA-ERR in Subents 002-008 was corrected into MISC.
